subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Analysis of Functions (Accumulation)
stimulus: The figure above shows the graph of the piecewise-linear function $f$. For $-4 \leq x \leq 12$, the function $g$ is defined by $g(x) = \int_{2}^{x} f(t)\,dt$.

parts.prompt: Does $g$ have a relative minimum, a relative maximum, or neither at $x = 10$? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Since $g'(x) = f(x)$, analyze the sign change of $f$ at $x = 10$ from the piecewise-linear graph.

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion: $g$ has a relative minimum at $x = 10$

sample_responses.commentary: Correct conclusion; $g'=f$ changes from negative to positive at $x=10$.
Incorrect: a relative max would require $g'=f$ to change from positive to negative. At $x=10$, $f$ changes from negative to positive, indicating a minimum.

scoring_points.description: Correct justification using sign change of $g'(x) = f(x)$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $g'(x)=f(x)$ changes from negative to positive at $x=10$

sample_responses.response: Since $g'(x) = f(x)$ and $f(x)$ changes from negative to positive at $x = 10$, $g$ has a relative minimum at $x = 10$.
sample_responses.commentary: Complete justification citing $g' = f$ and the sign change of $f$.
$g(10) = 0$ does not establish a minimum; $g(10)$ is the accumulated area and is not necessarily $0$. The justification must reference the sign change of $g' = f$.

required_elements: $g'(x) = f(x)$ changes from negative to positive at $x = 10$, so $g$ has a relative minimum there

parts.prompt: Does the graph of $g$ have a point of inflection at $x = 4$? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: A point of inflection occurs where $g''$ (equivalently $f'$) changes sign, i.e., where the slope of $f$ changes sign.

scoring_points.description: Correct answer (yes) with valid justification that $f'$ changes sign at $x = 4$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: yes; $g''=f'$ changes sign at $x=4$ (slope of $f$ changes)

sample_responses.response: Yes. Since $g''(x) = f'(x)$, a point of inflection occurs where the slope of $f$ changes sign. At $x=4$, the slope of $f$ changes (e.g., from positive to negative), so $g$ has a point of inflection there.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly ties $g''=f'$ to the change in slope of $f$ to justify the inflection point.
A zero of $f$ identifies where $g$ has a critical point, not an inflection point. An inflection point of $g$ requires $g'' = f'$ to change sign, not $f = 0$.

required_elements: Yes; $g''(x) = f'(x)$ changes sign at $x = 4$ because the slope of $f$ changes from positive to negative (or the slope of the line segment changes), so $g$ has a point of inflection at $x = 4$

parts.prompt: Find the absolute minimum value and the absolute maximum value of $g$ on the interval $-4 \leq x \leq 12$. Justify your answers.
parts.scoring_notes: Evaluate $g$ at critical points (where $f = 0$) and endpoints $x = -4$ and $x = 12$. $g$ values are found by computing net signed areas under $f$ from $x = 2$.

scoring_points.description: Correctly identifies all critical points on $[-4, 12]$ where $g'(x) = f(x) = 0$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: identifies zeros of $f$ on $[-4,12]$ as critical points of $g$, plus the endpoints

sample_responses.response: Critical points of $g$: where $g'(x) = f(x) = 0$. From the graph, zeros occur at $x = 0$, $x = 6$, $x = 10$ (and endpoints $x=-4$, $x=12$).
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies all critical and endpoint candidates for the absolute extrema.

sample_responses.response: Minimum occurs at $x = 10$ because $g$ has a relative minimum there.
sample_responses.commentary: The absolute minimum may or may not coincide with the relative minimum; all candidates (critical points and endpoints) must be evaluated and compared.

required_elements: Critical points where $f(x) = 0$: $x = -4$ is an endpoint; identify zeros of $f$ from the graph (e.g., $x = 0$, $x = 6$, $x = 10$) as candidates

scoring_points.description: Correct computation of $g$ values at the candidates (using net signed area)
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: evaluates $g$ at each candidate via net signed area $\int_2^x f(t)\,dt$

sample_responses.response: Computes $g(x) = \int_2^x f(t)\,dt$ at each candidate using the signed areas of triangles/trapezoids from the graph.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct approach: uses geometric areas under the piecewise-linear $f$ to evaluate $g$.

sample_responses.response: Evaluates $f$ at each candidate point instead of integrating
sample_responses.commentary: Evaluating $f(x)$ gives $g'(x)$, not $g(x)$. Must compute the definite integral $\int_2^x f(t)\,dt$ at each candidate.

required_elements: $g(x) = \int_2^x f(t)\,dt$ evaluated at each critical point and at both endpoints $x=-4$ and $x=12$ by computing net signed triangular/trapezoidal areas from the graph

scoring_points.description: Correct identification and value of the absolute minimum
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct absolute minimum value with location

sample_responses.response: Comparing all $g$ values: absolute minimum is $g(10) = [\text{computed value}]$.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the absolute minimum by comparing all candidates.

sample_responses.response: Absolute minimum is at $x = -4$ without computing $g(-4)$
sample_responses.commentary: Must compute the actual numerical value of $g$ at the minimum location.

required_elements: Absolute minimum value of $g$ stated with correct numerical value and correct $x$-location

scoring_points.description: Correct identification and value of the absolute maximum
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct absolute maximum value with location

sample_responses.response: Absolute maximum is $g(x_0) = [\text{correct value}]$ at $x = x_0$.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the absolute maximum by comparing all candidates.

sample_responses.response: Maximum is at $x = 6$ with $g(6) = 0$ (only checks one candidate)
sample_responses.commentary: Must compare all candidates; the maximum may occur at another location.

required_elements: Absolute maximum value of $g$ stated with correct numerical value and correct $x$-location

parts.prompt: For $-4 \leq x \leq 12$, find all intervals for which $g(x) \leq 0$.
parts.scoring_notes: Find where the net accumulated area $\int_2^x f(t)\,dt \leq 0$ by tracking sign of cumulative area from the graph of $f$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct interval(s) where net signed area $\int_2^x f(t)\,dt \leq 0$

sample_responses.response: From the area analysis: $g(x) \leq 0$ on $[-4, a]$ where $a$ is determined by where the net cumulative area turns non-negative.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct interval based on tracking the sign of accumulated area from $x=2$.
$g(x)$ is the accumulated area, not the value of $f(x)$. $g(x) \leq 0$ where the net integral from $2$ to $x$ is non-positive, which is independent of the sign of $f$ at $x$.

required_elements: Correct interval(s) determined by finding where the net signed area $\int_2^x f(t)\,dt \leq 0$

scoring_points.description: Correct justification referencing $g(x) = \int_2^x f(t)\,dt$ and the sign of accumulated area
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: work shows net area computation from piecewise graph to establish $g \leq 0$

sample_responses.response: Using signed areas from the graph: $g(x) = \int_2^x f(t)\,dt$. Computing areas at key points shows $g(x) \leq 0$ on the identified interval(s).
sample_responses.commentary: Correct justification showing the signed area work.
The sign of $g(x)$ depends on the cumulative integral from $x=2$ to $x$, not merely the local sign of $f(x)$.

required_elements: Work shows the net area computation from the piecewise-linear graph of $f$ to identify where $g \leq 0$
